item.title: Mothers and babies sleep on hospital floor in Lae
item.description: THE Angau Memorial Hospital’s postnatal ward is overcrowded, with up to 1,000 women admitted there each month, forcing some to recover on the floor because of the lack of bed and space. It is causing an increase in cases of sepsis among mothers and newborn babies.
item.content:encoded:
THE Angau Memorial Hospital’s postnatal ward is overcrowded, with up to 1,000 women admitted there each month, forcing some to recover on the floor because of the lack of bed and space.

It is causing an increase in cases of sepsis among mothers and newborn babies.

Dr Kamo Dumo, the hospital’s head of obstetrics and gynaecology, revealed this during a recent Morobe review session.

She said such poor conditions had become routine despite established standards of health care.

“It has become normal for us to have women on the floor with their babies. To this day, we are still having our women and babies on the floor,” she said.

The postnatal ward has only 40 beds. The labour ward recorded 9,114 deliveries in 2024 – around 25 births every day. “1,000 women are on the floor every month, and two or three mothers and babies share one mattress,” Dumo said.

“This is why our rates of neonatal and maternal sepsis are still rising.

“It is one of the contributing factors.”

Sepsis is the leading cause of maternal death at Angau.

Dumo said that the increase in sepsis was the result of delays at community level during transit, and within the hospital itself.

“It’s a combination of almost everything involved,” she said.

She said women arrive at Angau having already fought through the initial stages of infection. Some have undetected tuberculosis.

“Others present with breast infections, womb infections, or complications from prolonged labour.

They walked or travelled long distances to reach the hospital.

“Many of them come in with complications from difficult deliveries, and they travel miles with these problems just to get to Angau.

“By the time they arrive, the infection has had hours, sometimes days, to take hold.

“Overcrowded wards then compound the risk for mothers and newborns alike.

“Everything is delayed. That is how infection occurs. Second is the bleeding.”

Dumo said she appealed directly to stakeholders to treat the situation as a priority.

“Can this also be a matter of urgency on Morobe Provincial Health Authority (MoPHA) agenda?” she asked.

MoPHA  chief executive officer Dr Kipas Binga said the conditions at Angau should not be viewed as a failure of the hospital alone.

He said the facility was doing what it could against demand that has long outgrown its capacity.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG, Solomon Islands, Germany launch water initiative
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and Germany have launched a five-year initiative to protect fresh water resources from the impacts of climate change. The Protecting Freshwater Resources for Increased Climate Resilience in the Pacific Islands (PacFresH2O) was launched in Port Moresby. It focuses on enhancing water, sanitation, and hygiene systems and climate adaptation.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, and Germany have launched a five-year initiative to protect fresh water resources from the impacts of climate change.

The Protecting Freshwater Resources for Increased Climate Resilience in the Pacific Islands (PacFresH2O) was launched in Port Moresby. It focuses on enhancing water, sanitation, and hygiene systems and climate adaptation.

In PNG, the programme is focusing on Pomio and Rabaul in East New Britain.

Vice-Minister for National Planning Daniel Tindipu said that the negative impacts of climate change were no longer mere projections but a “lived experience”.

“There are saltwater intrusion into freshwater wells, more violent cyclones and storms damaging rainwater harvesting systems, unpredictable rainfall patterns disrupting agriculture and recharge of springs, and public health emergencies in the wake of every extreme weather event,” Tindipu said.

The initiative is funded by the German government through its International Climate Initiative.

Beate Grzeski, the German ambassador to Australia and the Pacific Island States, said: “Freshwater is fundamental to life, to health, and to economic development.”

“Yet, across the Pacific, water resources are increasingly under pressure. Climate change is already affecting rainfall patterns, increasing the frequency of droughts and floods, and placing growing strain on water, sanitation and hygiene systems.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Hawain Bridge takes shape as main steel girders installed
item.description: WORK on the Hawain Bridge project along the Wewak Coastal Highway in East Sepik has reached another significant level with the main girders and beams now completed, says Works and Highways Secretary Gibson Holemba. Holemba expressed satisfaction with the progress of the construction works, describing the completion of the main girders and beams as a major engineering achievement under the current
item.content:encoded:
WORK on the Hawain Bridge project along the Wewak Coastal Highway in East Sepik has reached another significant level with the main girders and beams now completed, says Works and Highways Secretary Gibson Holemba.

Holemba expressed satisfaction with the progress of the construction works, describing the completion of the main girders and beams as a major engineering achievement under the current Government’s Connect PNG programme.

He said the next phase of the project would focus on temporary works associated with the installation of the main steel tube truss, a critical component of the bridge structure.

Holemba said the completion of the bridge project was expected in October this year.

He said the Hawain Bridge project formed part of the Government’s broader effort to strengthen road connectivity, improve transportation access and support economic growth across Papua New Guinea.

“Once completed, the bridge is expected to significantly improve mobility for communities, businesses and travelers along the Wewak Coastal Highway corridor,” Holemba said.

The Department of Works and Highways reaffirmed its commitment to ensuring quality infrastructure delivery and timely progress on major national road projects connecting rural and urban communities throughout the country.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: No hope for next election, says O’Neill
item.description: THERE is no hope that General Election 2027 (GE27) will be fair and transparent, says former prime minister and Ialibu-Pangia MP Peter O’Neill. O’Neill said that three institutions of government were already in chaos.
item.content:encoded:
THERE is no hope that General Election 2027 (GE27) will be fair and transparent, says former prime minister and Ialibu-Pangia MP Peter O’Neill.

O’Neill said that three institutions of government were already in chaos.

“The Electoral Commission is underfunded. The Defence Force is now into all sorts of recruitment and scandal after scandal,” he said.

“The police force hierarchy is in complete mess. The rank and file have got no respect for the commissioner.

“It doesn’t matter how much money you put into these institutions. When the leadership of those institutions are not stable, not transparent, and not working, that organisation will not work,” he said.

O’Neill said there is no hope that 2027 elections will be fair and transparent.

“If people recall, the 2022 elections were a complete fuss. Much of the counting was highly compromised.”

“Most counting was not completed. In fact, three seats were declared under special circumstances. Worst possible outcomes in the history of our nation.

“That Marape Government has never run an election that is fair, transparent, and in the best interest of our nation. His own electorate has never had a transparent election,” O’Neill said.

“If you have a leader who has been elected under those circumstances leading the nation to an election, you have something to worry about.”

O’Neill said the Pangu government led by Marape had now been in power for over seven years, and it was clear that this government did not care about the people.

“They are arrogant. From the decisions that they are making today, they are arrogant, and their whole aim is to just remain in power at any cost, at any cost.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: AJ: No safety worries
item.description: PNG Chiefs’ latest signing, Alex Johnston (Chief 002), has strongly rejected ongoing safety concerns surrounding Papua New Guinea, declaring that Port Moresby could become “the safest place in the world” for rugby league players. “Safety is not my concern at all,” Johnston said. “The only concern is if you’re going to shake too many hands or get too many photos. They’ll absolutely love you. They’r
item.content:encoded:
PNG Chiefs’ latest signing, Alex Johnston (Chief 002), has strongly rejected ongoing safety concerns surrounding Papua New Guinea, declaring that Port Moresby could become “the safest place in the world” for rugby league players.

“Safety is not my concern at all,” Johnston said. “The only concern is if you’re going to shake too many hands or get too many photos. They’ll absolutely love you. They’re not going to do anything to you at all.”

His remarks come amid continued debate in Australia over player welfare, security, and living conditions ahead of the Chiefs’ NRL debut. Johnston, the competition’s all-time leading try-scorer, argued that many of the fears being raised publicly do not reflect the reality he has personally experienced in PNG.

The comments directly challenge growing criticism from sections of the Australian media, where questions persist about arrangements for players and their families once the Chiefs enter the competition.

The issue has become one of the most sensitive talking points surrounding the 19th NRL franchise, particularly as the club pursues marquee signings from Australia.

Johnston believes much of the skepticism overlooks the unique bond between Papua New Guinea and rugby league.

He described PNG supporters as some of the most passionate fans in the world, saying their love for the game and appreciation for players creates an atmosphere unlike anywhere else.

Looking ahead to the Chiefs’ debut in 2028, Johnston predicted an extraordinary occasion. “The first game will be crazy. To have an actual NRL side, I can only imagine it’ll be that and more,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Backlash reflects Chiefs’ job
item.description: THE backlash on PNG Chiefs’ tax-free player incentives is quickly becoming a hot topic in the National Rugby League (NRL) and it only proves one thing – the club is now being taken seriously. “I think it reflects the job that the Chiefs are doing, that people are now saying it’s unfair because it means that we’re doing the right thing,” Chiefs general manager (football) Michael Chammas told The Na
item.content:encoded:
THE backlash on PNG Chiefs’ tax-free player incentives is quickly becoming a hot topic in the National Rugby League (NRL) and it only proves one thing – the club is now being taken seriously.

“I think it reflects the job that the Chiefs are doing, that people are now saying it’s unfair because it means that we’re doing the right thing,” Chiefs general manager (football) Michael Chammas told The National.

“It means that we’re getting the right players and people are now taking us seriously.”

Chammas’ comments come after weeks of criticism from sections of the Australian rugby league media following the Chiefs’ successful signing of superstar Jarome Luai ahead of the club’s planned 2028 NRL entry.

The signing immediately triggered concern among rival clubs, who fear the Chiefs’ tax-free contracts could give them a major advantage in attracting elite talent.

Under the PNG Government-backed arrangement, players and officials linked to the Chiefs are expected to receive tax-free salaries, a benefit publicly confirmed earlier this year by Prime Minister James Marape.

The policy has created anger among several clubs and commentators in Australia, with some describing the arrangement as a “salary cap rort” and an uneven playing field.

Much of the frustration has come from Perth, where coach Mal Meninga and club officials have reportedly pushed for their own recruitment concessions after struggling to secure marquee signings.

Unlike the Chiefs, the Bears were not granted major salary cap exemptions or tax-free arrangements, despite entering the competition earlier in 2027.

The result has created a growing divide between the two expansion clubs.

“Everyone’s known for a long time that this is going to be the case with the tax-free,” he said. “So I think it’s important we just keep moving forward and getting good people and build the team to be competitive because that’s what the people of PNG expect,” he added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Johnston clears air on signing drama
item.description: PNG Chiefs bound Alex Johnston has dismissed claims that he attempted to sabotage fellow superstar Jarome Luai during the club’s early recruitment process, revealing the confusion came from believing his own deal had already been finalised. The South Sydney Rabbitohs winger has now spoken publicly about the controversy surrounding his early comments about joining the Chiefs.
item.content:encoded:
PNG Chiefs bound Alex Johnston has dismissed claims that he attempted to sabotage fellow superstar Jarome Luai during the club’s early recruitment process, revealing the confusion came from believing his own deal had already been finalised.

The South Sydney Rabbitohs winger has now spoken publicly about the controversy surrounding his early comments about joining the Chiefs.

Some critics questioned whether Johnston’s announcement was deliberately timed to place pressure on the Chiefs or influence Luai’s negotiations.

But Johnston strongly denied those claims, saying there was never any intention to create problems for the club or interfere with another player’s signing.

“There’s no intent to sabotage anything,” Johnston told the Australian Media on Wednesday during the official announcement of his signing. “I genuinely thought I’d signed.”

The Kumul No.294 revealed he had already informed close family members about the move while attending a family gathering and later shared the news with teammates and coaching staff at training.

He said he had spoken openly with South Sydney coach Wayne Bennett and football staff because he believed the deal was complete. However, the public reaction exposed that the agreement had not yet been officially confirmed.

While Johnston did not directly confirm whether mediation talks took place with Chiefs officials, his comments suggest the matter was handled internally before escalating further.

Every signing linked to the franchise is now being heavily scrutinized, especially involving high-profile names.

Despite the controversy, Johnston also opened up about the difficult decision to leave the Bunnies after spending his entire career in the famous red and green jersey.

The all-time leading try scorer admitted he once dreamed of becoming a lifelong one-club player.

“I wanted to be in the red and green for my whole career,” he said.

Although he remains focused on the next year and a half with the Rabbitohs, Johnston acknowledged that the final goodbye could be emotional for both himself and the club’s supporters.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: AJ eyes World Cup
item.description: THE upcoming Rugby League World Cup set for October in Port Moresby could become a defining moment for the PNG Kumuls, says winger Alex Johnston. The South Sydney Rabbitohs flyer said the Kumuls continued to improve every year and believes the squad can go beyond their previous World Cup achievements in October.
item.content:encoded:
THE upcoming Rugby League World Cup set for October in Port Moresby could become a defining moment for the PNG Kumuls, says winger Alex Johnston.

The South Sydney Rabbitohs flyer said the Kumuls continued to improve every year and believes the squad can go beyond their previous World Cup achievements in October.

The PNG Chiefs inaugural player no.002 said PNG’s strong rugby league culture could become one of the Kumuls’ biggest weapons during the tournament.

“We’ve been building something strong,” Johnston said. “Since I’ve been playing with the Kumuls, we’ve been going up and up.”

Johnston said Kumuls World Cup campaign will not only be about football, but also about inspiring people and giving back to the country.

The excitement surrounding the Chiefs has also added more energy ahead of the international tournament.

Johnston said he is already looking forward to potentially facing Samoan International Jarome Luai.

“This time it’s going to be amazing being here in PNG,” he said.

Widely regarded as one of rugby league’s greatest try scorers, Johnston said he once dreamed of being a one-club player with South Sydney Rabbitohs and building a lasting legacy at the club.

Inspired by former Rabbitohs captain John Sutton, Johnston said he always wanted to reach 300 games and leave behind something special at South Sydney.

Now, after achieving many of those goals, Johnston believes joining the Chiefs gives him the chance to build another chapter in his career while helping shape rugby league history in PNG.

“My move is not about leaving behind my South Sydney legacy, but adding something new to it through the Chiefs project and the continued rise of rugby league over there,” he added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Preparing for global NRL stage
item.description: THE recent signing of National Rugby League (NRL) player Jarome Luai is expected to attract fans from Australia and beyond to Port Moresby, the city hosting NRL games in 2028. However, Port Moresby is currently unfit to host such events.
item.content:encoded:
THE recent signing of National Rugby League (NRL) player Jarome Luai is expected to attract fans from Australia and beyond to Port Moresby, the city hosting NRL games in 2028.

However, Port Moresby is currently unfit to host such events.

The city suffers from widespread filth, as many people, including professionals, chew betel nut and spit indiscriminately, spreading germs and increasing the risk of tuberculosis.

While cities like Madang and Lae have banned public chewing, the National Capital District Commission (NCDC) and local MPs have taken no action.

Travellers regularly see streets littered with betel nut stains, plastic bottles, and cans, particularly in Gordon, Waigani, and Boroko.

Vendors operate with little oversight, and professionals – from bankers to public servants – participate in the practice, tarnishing the city’s image.

Authorities, including NCDC, MPs, and the Health Department, must act before next year to clean the streets and ban public chewing.

Residents can chew at home, but public spaces should remain clean.

Without immediate intervention, visiting NRL players and fans could face health risks.

Port Moresby must implement a visible cleanup campaign and enforce regulations to ensure the capital presents a safe and welcoming environment ahead of the 2028 games.

THERE is growing excitement over Papua New Guinea Chiefs joining National Rugby League (NRL) in 2028, but expectations must remain realistic.

NRL clubs are professional businesses responsible for generating revenue and managing expenses under strict salary caps. 

The Australian Government’s A$600 million (about K1.88 billion) support package is tied to the Defence Treaty and lasts ten years, while PNG has already committed K200 million for infrastructure.

The long-term success of PNG Chiefs will depend on strong leadership, sound management, and freedom from political interference.

Jerome Luai’s signing has already attracted attention across the NRL, and tax-free incentives may lure more stars.

The real challenge is building a sustainable and competitive club for the future.

Martin Mintai Waure Mondia Village, Kere Du

DESPITE the excitement around Jerome Luai joining the Papua New Guinea Chiefs last week, it’s worth reflecting on the bigger picture.

PNG is still a developing country, and while Luai is a talented footballer, the decision of the Government – not the franchise – to pay for a private jet to fly him and his family to Port Moresby to sign a three-year tax-free contract was excessive and in poor taste.

Regular business-class flights are available, making this lavish gesture unnecessary.

Luai arguably should not have accepted it.

The spectacle was widely reported in Australian sports media, and now other Australian players are showing interest in PNG’s tax-free opportunities.

This raises concerns for local talent: every international star who joins the Chiefs reduces opportunities for homegrown player’s eager to break into professional rugby league.

PNG has many skilled rugby league players performing well internationally. Exposure to top-level competition abroad is crucial for improving their skills, confidence, and career prospects.

The Chiefs franchise is an excellent platform to showcase local talent and develop PNG rugby league.

For the team and the country to benefit, careful consideration must be given to balancing international signings with nurturing domestic players and giving them the opportunities they deserve.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Starlink brings digital age to villages
item.description: A POWERFUL human interest story from Rigo in Central reminds us that real development reaches even Papua New Guinea’s most isolated communities. Recently, journalist Malum Nalu shared updates from Majorie, with the PNG Tribal Foundation, about Dorobisoro village near the Owen Stanley Range foothills.
item.content:encoded:
A POWERFUL human interest story from Rigo in Central reminds us that real development reaches even Papua New Guinea’s most isolated communities.

Recently, journalist Malum Nalu shared updates from Majorie, with the PNG Tribal Foundation, about Dorobisoro village near the Owen Stanley Range foothills.

Starlink internet has now connected villagers to the outside world for the first time in history.

While urban Papua New Guinea takes online communication for granted, remote communities have long faced expensive, unreliable, and difficult access.

Villagers were reportedly overwhelmed with joy as they shared video, audio, and pictures beyond their mountains for the first time.

The impact goes beyond communication – it opens doors for education, healthcare, emergency response, and national inclusion.

Students can access learning materials, health workers can seek professional advice during emergencies, and families can stay in touch more easily.

Villagers can coordinate during disasters, disease outbreaks, or conflicts, where timely information can save lives.

In the future, farmers and small businesses may also benefit from digital banking, market access, and online training.

Credit goes those who shared this inspiring story.

Dorobisoro proves that no part of PNG should remain disconnected in the digital age.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Embrace Starlink and open competition
item.description: FOR too long, Papua New Guinea has been held back by limited internet options, high prices, and poor coverage, leaving most rural communities disconnected. Digital transformation and inclusive development are impossible when a student in Telefomin or a health worker in Kaintiba cannot access a reliable signal.
item.content:encoded:
FOR too long, Papua New Guinea has been held back by limited internet options, high prices, and poor coverage, leaving most rural communities disconnected.

Digital transformation and inclusive development are impossible when a student in Telefomin or a health worker in Kaintiba cannot access a reliable signal.

Satellite internet, like Starlink, changes that overnight. Claims that Starlink must be stopped to protect “certain interests” reveal whose interests are prioritised – and it’s certainly not ordinary Papua New Guineans.

Mothers running online small-to-medium enterprises in Goroka, teachers downloading classroom resources in Vanimo, and hospitals relying on telehealth all benefit from better connectivity.

If existing providers cannot compete on price and service, the solution is improvement, not exclusion.

Delays mean lost opportunities for education, commerce, emergency response, and governance.

Starlink offers redundancy when fiber and towers fail during cyclones or Highlands Highway disruptions.

Regulatory concerns should be handled transparently by National Information and Communications Technology Authority or the Independent Consumer and Competition Commission, not through secretive directives that undermine public trust.

Digicel’s entry lowered prices and expanded coverage; Starlink can bring the same benefits to the 80 per cent of Papua New Guineans outside coverage. PNG should decide based on the needs of its people, not entrenched interests.

Let Starlink proceed, let the market work, and let PNG connect.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Waghi Mek Plantations revives green gold
item.description: IT is inspiring to see the people of Jiwaka reclaiming pride in Waghi Mek Plantations Limited (WMPL) with modest support from the Government. Kudos to WMPL chief executive officer Mark Munnul, his management team, ward councillors, and presidents for their leadership and unwavering support.
item.content:encoded:
IT is inspiring to see the people of Jiwaka reclaiming pride in Waghi Mek Plantations Limited (WMPL) with modest support from the Government.

Kudos to WMPL chief executive officer Mark Munnul, his management team, ward councillors, and presidents for their leadership and unwavering support.

With no major industries to boost local revenue, reviving these plantations is crucial for socio-economic development.

More than 10 plantations have been restored, and the first green bean coffee shipments have reached overseas markets.

Manually harvested beans from plantations and village smallholders create a sustainable production network, strengthening the local economy and directly improving livelihoods.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Building support system for victims of violence in Jiwaka
item.description: My whole village turned against me after a single accusation. I ran for my life. I cannot go home. I cannot reclaim my land. If I go back, they will kill me.” In Papua New Guinea’s Highlands, that is not a rare horror.
item.content:encoded:
My whole village turned against me after a single accusation. I ran for my life. I cannot go home. I cannot reclaim my land. If I go back, they will kill me.”

In Papua New Guinea’s Highlands, that is not a rare horror.

It is a lived reality for women caught in the grip of sorcery accusation-related violence, where the attack itself is often only the beginning.

As for survivors of domestic abuse and sexual violence in the area, after the violence comes another fight: finding care quickly, finding it safely, and finding it before fear, stigma and silence close in.

PNG’s latest Demographic and Health Survey (2016-2018) found that 56 per cent of women aged 15 to 49 had experienced physical violence, while 28 per cent  had experienced sexual violence.

Among ever-married women, 63 per cent  had experienced emotional, physical or sexual violence by a spouse.

Even then, many survivors remain silent.

Thirty-nine per cent  of women who had experienced physical or sexual violence had neither sought help nor told anyone.

In the Highlands, the burden is even heavier, with 31.8 per cent  of women aged 15 to 49 reporting sexual violence.

Sorcery accusation-related violence adds another layer of terror.

A 2024 paper by the PNG National Research Institute recorded 1,039 cases affecting 1,554 persons across four provinces between 2016 and 2020.

Jiwaka is considered one of the country’s four hotspots.

Much of this violence never reaches formal reporting systems.

Many of those targeted are women and girls.

In Jiwaka, Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) works alongside the Provincial Health Authority to expand access to safe, confidential, respectful and free care for survivors of all forms of violence, and to strengthen essential health services across the province.

To strengthen the quality of care and improve community access to essential services, teams assessed health facilities across all three districts to identify gaps in service delivery and priority areas for support.

Based on these findings, MSF delivered several training sessions in nine facilities and trained 164 health workers from September to December last year, aiming to improve clinical capacity and ensure safer, more consistent care.

To increase public awareness and help communities better navigate available services, teams also conducted 245 health talk sessions in Anglip-South Waghi communities, including Minj Health Centre, reaching 1,998 persons while village health volunteers then led nearly 150 community sessions reaching 2,239 persons.

In September last year, the Family Support Centre at Minj began operating with MSF support, providing survivors with a clearer path from first medical care to follow-up appointments.

In cases of rape or severe assault, time matters.

In the first 72 hours, a survivor may need treatment for injuries, emergency contraception, HIV prevention, protection from infection and urgent psychosocial support.

That is why the Family Support Centre matters.

It is meant to make those first hours count and reduce the risk that survivors are lost between services.

“A referral pathway is not a chart on a wall,” said Rachel Wehrung, MSF project coordinator.

“It is a promise that a survivor will not be sent from place to place, forced to repeat her story, relive her trauma, and face the same backlash again.

“If that promise is broken, the pathway becomes another form of harm.”

A referral pathway, in plain terms, is whether help actually leads somewhere.

Whether a woman in crisis meets someone trained, discreet and ready to act, or is left to carry her trauma from office to office until exhaustion, fear or shame takes over.

In Jiwaka, that first link is often not a doctor.

“I became a village health volunteer because I have seen what violence does to our sisters and mothers,” said Yen, a local volunteer.
